text: The surgeon observes inflammatory adhesions of the omentum attached to the tumor and decides to leave them in place.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, progressing through the tissue layers in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon carefully dissects laterally along the back of the mesorectum, ensuring nerve protection during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and gently sweeps it downward, ensuring it is safely moved out of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node located at the junction of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and the aorta.
The surgeon resects a separate specimen, identified as the highest lymph node at the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) junction.
The surgeon clears tissue around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) just distal to its takeoff from the aorta and proximal to the left colic artery.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, progressing through the tissue planes in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection, extending it laterally along the colon, using surgical instruments to separate tissue layers.
The surgeon exposes the gonadal vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the medial dissection, joining the medial plane with a single incision.
A sponge is placed over the ureter to protect it during the dissection.
The surgeon gently separates the sigmoid mesentery from the sidewall, with only a thin cell layer still connecting them, allowing for easy detachment.
The surgeon clears the rectosigmoid mesentery, separating it from surrounding tissues using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon prepares to perform an intracorporeal sewn anastomosis, indicating the transition to connecting the bowel segments within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon prepares to exteriorize the specimen through a fan and steel incision after completing the intracorporeal anastomosis and clearing the rectosigmoid mesentery.
The surgeon performs proximal and distal division of the bowel segments in preparation for an intracorporeal sewn anastomosis.
The surgeon aligns the anastomosis by sewing the posterior outer row using a V-lock suture.
The surgeon creates incisions in the well-vascularized rectum, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon examines the intervening segment of rectum located between the staple line and the otomy.
The surgeon observes the rectum, noting that it typically enhances with indocyanine green (ICG) and appears pink and well-perfused, although occasionally it may appear dusky.
The surgeon amputates the staple line, removing the section of bowel tissue along the line of staples.
The surgeon joins two bowel segments, converting the connection into an end-to-end anastomosis, ensuring the bowel appears healthy and well-perfused.
The surgeon aligns the bowel segments for an end-to-end anastomosis, ensuring proper orientation for joining the healthy, perfused bowel.
The surgeon observes improved perfusion in the surgical area, indicating enhanced blood flow to the tissues.
The surgeon begins sewing a single layer end-to-end anastomosis, aligning the bowel ends for connection.
The surgeon places a trio silk seromuscular suture posteriorly in the midline to align and stabilize the anastomosis.
The surgeon begins sewing the end-to-end anastomosis from the left-hand side, following their usual practice.
The surgeon observes the healthy bowel with a wide open lumen and zero tension, indicating a secure end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon is threading a suture over the top, positioned towards the patient's right side.
The surgeon brings two sutures towards the middle of the surgical field, likely preparing to secure or adjust the anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully imbricates the mucosal layers, ensuring that only the serosal surfaces are in contact with each other.
The surgeon uses a back-and-forth suturing technique to close the anastomosis, ensuring there is no bowing or folding of the tissue.

The surgeon ensures the lip of the tissue is securely dunked and imbricated by repeatedly passing sutures back and forth in the area. The two ends of the sutures are then tied together to complete the closure.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the newly sutured section of the bowel, checking for any visible signs of fluid leakage at the anastomosis site.
The surgeon introduces irrigant posteriorly to facilitate the release of trapped air bubbles within the surgical site.
